question: A process integrates with a callback-style payment gateway whose response can arrive minutes — sometimes hours — later. Which BPMN element best fits this request/response, and why?

options.text: A send task followed by a receive task — you control the correlation ID and don't rely on an internal job instance key over long waits.
A service task — Zeebe's generated job key is the simplest correlation, and waiting hours on an open job is fine.
A service task — message buffering will queue the late response until the process catches up.
A send task only — fire-and-forget removes the need to wait for the callback at all.

a.text: Correct per the docs' summary table: use send + receive 'when the response will take time (> some seconds), or you need a correlation ID you can control.' Over long waits, operational restarts can change a job key and break correlation, so an owned correlation ID is safer.

b.text: Service tasks are fine for millisecond-range request/response, but the docs warn that cancelling and restarting an instance creates a new job key, breaking outstanding response correlation — a real risk over hours.

c.text: Message buffering is a feature of the messages concept used by send + receive tasks, not of service tasks. Service tasks do not buffer messages.

d.text: A send-task-only pattern is fire-and-forget — the process won't observe the callback at all, which fails the actual business requirement.

explanation: For long-running responses the docs recommend the send + receive task combo because (a) you own the correlation ID and don't depend on the engine-internal job instance key, and (b) message buffering and explicit correlation are part of the messages concept built into Zeebe. Service tasks are best when responses return within milliseconds.
